title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman accuses Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat of repeatedly maneuvering to kill SB13 (the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act) while authoring weaker substitute bills (SB195) to pacify pro-life voters. The article frames Treat's submission to Roe as a hypocritical 'doctrine of men' that violates both God's law and the Constitution.

questions.question: How was SB13 actually killed in committee, and who was involved in the decision?
questions.answer: Senate Health and Human Services chairman Jason Smalley convened a private meeting with Republican committee members (excluding the bill's author Joseph Silk) to count votes. When it became clear there weren't enough votes to kill the bill openly, Smalley conferred with Treat and unilaterally denied SB13 a hearing.
questions.quote: When the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (OK SB13) was introduced, Senate Health and Human Services chairman Jason Smalley (R-Stroud) held a private meeting with his Republican committee members except for SB13 author Joseph Silk (R-Broken Bow) to determine whether there were enough votes to kill it, according to a source close to the situation. When it became apparent there weren't, Smalley, after deliberating with Treat, denied SB13 a hearing.

questions.question: What grassroots tactics did abolitionists use to pressure Smalley and Treat after SB13 was denied a hearing?
questions.answer: Activists rented billboards naming Smalley as the man keeping abortion legal, distributed thousands of doorhangers throughout his district, and drove email and phone-call campaigns to both Treat's and Smalley's offices.
questions.quote: Billboards were rented sharing with Oklahomans the message that Smalley is the one keeping abortion legal in Oklahoma. Thousands of doorhangers were placed on front doors in his district. Large amounts of emails and phone calls were sent to the offices of both Treat and Smalley voicing displeasure.

questions.question: What was SB195, and why does Silberman consider it a cowardly substitute for SB13?
questions.answer: SB195 was a trigger bill that would only restore pre-Roe Oklahoma abortion law if Roe were first overturned by the Supreme Court or amended out of the U.S. Constitution. Silberman calls this the move of someone who pretends to care about babies but is committed to obeying the federal courts.
questions.quote: Treat quickly authored and introduced OK SB195, a bill which would trigger a return to pre-Roe state abortion law upon Roe being overturned or abortion being abolished in the US Constitution. Killing an abolition bill and putting forward a bill like this is what someone does when they pretend to care about babies being murdered but are dead set on licking the boots of the feds.

questions.question: What is the constitutional basis the article gives for states refusing to obey Roe?
questions.answer: Silberman argues that states are not bound by court opinions that are themselves illegal or immoral. He claims states are both constitutionally and morally obligated to ignore rulings like Roe, and links to a separate piece on nullification and interposition.
questions.quote: Contrary to popular belief, states are under no obligation to obey arbitrary, illegal court opinions, especially opinions as depraved as Roe's legalization of murder. In fact, states are constitutionally and morally obligated to ignore such rulings.

questions.question: How did Treat amend SB195 after abolitionists rejected the first version, and why is that even worse?
questions.answer: Treat changed SB195 into a ballot referral asking voters to amend the state constitution to clarify it doesn't allow abortion, while explicitly preserving enforcement of the Supreme Court's pro-abortion opinion. Silberman calls this weaker than the original because it lets Treat appear to defer to voters while ensuring nothing actually changes.
questions.quote: This time, he amended SB195 so it gives Oklahoma voters a chance to vote on amending the State Constitution to clarify that it doesn't allow for abortion. The bill specifies that the Supreme Court's opinion that states must allow abortion will still be enforced. This is even weaker than the previous version of the bill, but for Treat, it represents washing his hands of the matter and pretending that he's putting abortion's abolition in the hands of voters when he's doing no such thing.

questions.question: Why does the article frame Treat's deference to the Supreme Court as a violation of Matthew 15?
questions.answer: Silberman casts judicial supremacy as a man-made tradition that voids the law of God, paralleling the Pharisees Jesus rebuked for elevating their traditions over divine commands. By killing SB13 to honor Roe, Treat allegedly enshrines a 'commandment of men' as doctrine.
questions.quote: The concept of obsequious submission to the Supreme Court up to and including obeying its opinion that murder should be legal is an evil, unconstitutional "commandment of men" which Treat holds as doctrine. By killing SB13, Treat is making the tradition of judicial supremacy his doctrine while voiding God's word and the US Constitution.

questions.question: How does the article connect Jesus's greatest-commandment teaching to legislative votes on abortion?
questions.answer: Silberman invokes Matthew 22 to argue that obeying Roe is incompatible with both loving God (treating His law as worthless) and loving neighbor (upholding rulings that classify some neighbors as subhuman and killable). The vote becomes a direct test of the two greatest commandments.
questions.quote: One does not love God if they treat His law as worthless while treating the Supreme Court's whims as unimpeachable and supreme. They do not love their neighbor if they help uphold evil rulings that their neighbors are subhuman and can be legally murdered.

questions.question: What is the rhetorical irony Silberman draws from Treat's earlier use of Matthew?
questions.answer: Treat himself once cited Matthew to call colleagues who opposed a tax increase 'cowards.' Silberman turns that label back on Treat by pointing to two other Matthew passages he says Treat needs to read.
questions.quote: Treat once quoted from the Gospel of Matthew while calling his colleagues in the legislature who opposed a tax increase cowards. There are two other passages from Matthew which Treat needs to read.

questions.question: What concrete action does the article say Treat must take to redeem his record?
questions.answer: Silberman tells Treat to bring SB13 directly to the Senate floor for a vote and to actively work to pass it. If he does, abolitionists will welcome him and forgive his prior actions; if not, he will be remembered as the man who kept abortion legal in Oklahoma.
questions.quote: Senator Treat must stop hating his neighbor and stop nullifying the Constitution and God's word by obeying Roe. He must bring SB13, the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, directly to the Senate floor for a vote and do everything in his power to make sure it passes.

questions.question: What political bind does Silberman say Treat is trying to navigate?
questions.answer: Treat is portrayed as trying to keep abortion legal while still appearing pro-life to voters who are catching on to GOP lip-service. The article frames every successive bill (SB195 and its amendment) as another attempt to thread that needle without ever genuinely ending abortion.
questions.quote: Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat (R-Oklahoma City) is going into overdrive to keep abortion legal while maintaining pro-life voters.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Directly linked from this article's opening line and provides the broader cast of GOP leaders blocking SB13.

related.title: Uncovered Letter Exposes Plan to Keep Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Published the same month and documents further evidence of the coordinated effort by Treat, Smalley, and allies to suppress SB13.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: By the same author, expands the nullification/interposition argument that Silberman gestures at when claiming states may ignore Roe.

related.title: Free the States Statement on Sen. Jason Smalley's Resignation
related.reason: Follows the Smalley side of the SB13 story to its conclusion, showing the consequences of the obstruction described here.

related.title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
related.reason: Continues the Treat-vs-SB13 narrative a year later, refuting Treat's evolving public talking points against the bill.

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Companion theological response to other religious leaders aligning with the same status-quo posture Treat is accused of holding.

related.title: Motion to Bring SB13 to Senate Floor Coming Tomorrow! Email Your Senator!
related.reason: Operationalizes the exact ask Silberman closes this article with: forcing SB13 to a floor vote.
